1.pericope_label: She: I Am a Rose of Sharon
1.synthesis:
The brief opening exchange of chapter 2 is a masterpiece of compressed mutual recognition. The woman begins with a self-description that is simultaneously modest and inviting: 'I am a rose of Sharon, a lily of the valleys' (v. 1). The Hebrew ḥăḇaṣṣelet is not certainly the rose but possibly a narcissus, crocus, or tulip — a common wildflower of the coastal plain of Sharon. The 'lily of the valleys' (šôšannāh) similarly suggests an ordinary meadow flower rather than a rare cultivar. The self-description is one of common beauty: she is beautiful as the wildflowers of the field, accessible, unpretentious, scattered everywhere rather than rare and cultivated.

The beloved immediately elevates her comparison: 'As a lily among brambles, so is my love among the young women' (v. 2). He accepts her flower-comparison but reframes it — she is not just a lily, she is a lily among brambles. The surrounding 'young women' (bānôt) are not vilified but simply contrasted: beside them, she stands out as a lily stands out among thorns. Love sees with particular clarity, rendering the beloved uniquely visible in a way that does not require the devaluation of others but simply perceives a particular beauty that overcomes comparison.

Her responsive comparison in v. 3 — 'As an apple tree among the trees of the forest, so is my beloved among the young men. In his shadow I sat with great delight, and his fruit was sweet to my taste' — mirrors his: he is a productive, fragrant fruit tree among the wild, unproductive trees of the forest. But she adds something he did not: she sits in his shadow with delight and eats his fruit. The relationship is not merely mutual appreciation but a resting-in and a receiving-from: she is not only the object of his gaze but the beneficiary of his shelter and provision.
1.historical_context: The plain of Sharon was the fertile coastal plain between the Carmel ridge and the Judean hills, known for its wildflowers in spring. The apple tree (tappûaḥ, v. 3) is disputed — some argue it is a quince or apricot since apples were rare in ancient Canaan — but the point is the tree's provision of shade and fruit in contrast to the unproductive forest trees. The mutuality of comparison in vv. 1–3 — she compares herself, he refines the comparison, she mirrors it back with an added dimension — is the characteristic dialogic structure of the Song.
1.literary_structure: The three verses form a brief, interlocking exchange: woman's self-description (v. 1) → man's responsive reframing (v. 2) → woman's mirror-comparison with added dimension of resting and receiving (v. 3). The escalating movement — common wildflower → lily among brambles → apple tree with shade and fruit — enacts the Song's characteristic dynamic: each declaration of love elevates and enriches the prior one. Love does not remain at the level of initial comparison but deepens through responsive exchange.
1.theological_application: The woman's initial self-description as a common wildflower — not rare, not cultivated, but simply beautiful in the ordinary way of creation — and the beloved's response that she is a lily among brambles enacts the theological dynamic of covenantal election: the beloved is not chosen because of exceptional rarity but loved with a specificity that makes her uniquely visible. The resting in the apple tree's shadow (v. 3) — 'I sat with great delight' — is the image of the rest that love provides: the relationship is not only longing but arrival, not only seeking but finding and dwelling in the shade of the one who shelters.
1.christology: The rose of Sharon and lily of the valleys became one of Christian hymnody's most beloved applications to Christ himself — a reading which, while not exegetically native to the text, captures the Song's christological depth: Christ is the flower of the divine field, the one who grows from the root of Jesse (Isa 11:1), unassuming in appearance yet uniquely beautiful. The apple tree under whose shadow the beloved rests with great delight (v. 3) is read as Christ under whose cross the church finds shelter: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28). In him the soul finds both shade from judgment and the sweet fruit of his grace.

4.synthesis:
The beloved's 'banquet hall' — 'He brought me to the banqueting house, and his banner over me was love' (v. 4) — is one of the Song's most celebrated images. The 'house of wine' (bêt hayyāyin) is both the specific setting of a feast and the broader atmosphere of intoxicating joy that the beloved creates. The 'banner' (degel) was a military standard raised over the army to identify it — here it is love itself raised over the woman as her identification and protection. To be 'under the banner of love' is to be publicly claimed, visibly loved, marked as belonging to the one who raised that banner.

The physical description of vv. 5–6 — 'Sustain me with raisins; refresh me with apples, for I am sick with love. His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me' — portrays the woman in a state of love-overwhelming: she asks for sustaining foods (raisins and apples were considered restorative) because love has made her physically weak. Love in the Song is not a mild sentiment but an overwhelming force that requires sustenance to bear. The beloved's embrace of vv. 6 — left hand under her head, right hand embracing — is the complete encircling that moves from the physical to the symbolic: she is held on every side.

The refrain of v. 7 — 'I adjure you, O daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles or the hinds of the field, that you not stir up or awaken love until it pleases' — will recur at 3:5 and 8:4, forming the Song's most important structural refrain. The adjuration by the gazelles and hinds — wild creatures of grace and freedom — grounds the warning in natural rather than religious oath. Love has its own timing (šetteḥpāṣ — until it pleases, until it wants to); it cannot and should not be forced or rushed. The refrain is the Song's pastoral wisdom about love: it comes in its own time, and its timing must be honored.
4.historical_context: The 'house of wine' (v. 4) was a literal wine-hall or banqueting chamber in the ancient Near East, used for royal feasts. The raisins (ʾăšîšôt) of v. 5 were pressed grape cakes — energy-dense foods used for restoration. The 'I am sick with love' (ḥôlat ʾahăbāh ʾānî) is a state recognized in ANE love poetry: love as a condition that requires medical attention, an overwhelming of normal faculties. The adjuration formula of v. 7 — 'I adjure you by the gazelles or the hinds' — avoids using the divine name while still invoking a solemn oath through nature.
4.literary_structure: The section moves from the banquet experience (v. 4) → the physical overwhelming of love requiring sustenance (v. 5) → the beloved's embrace (v. 6) → the refrain warning against premature awakening of love (v. 7). The movement from exhilaration to overwhelm to physical holding to warning establishes the pattern that will recur: love is both glorious and dangerous, requiring the protection of appropriate timing and context.
4.theological_application: The refrain 'do not stir up or awaken love until it pleases' (v. 7) is the Song's most explicit pastoral counsel: love has its own timing and must not be forced or manipulated into premature expression. This is wisdom about the nature of genuine love — it cannot be manufactured, controlled, or rushed without deforming it. The contemporary application is direct: the culture of manufactured intimacy and premature sexual experience disrespects the nature of love as described by the Song, which requires patience, timing, and the protective context of covenant commitment.
4.christology: The banner of love over the beloved (v. 4) is read in the Christian tradition as the cross of Christ raised over the church: love publicly proclaimed, publicly claimed. 'May I never boast except in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ' (Gal 6:14) — the cross is the banner under which the church is identified. The 'sick with love' of v. 5 is the soul's longing for Christ, which Paul describes in similar terms: 'I have a desire to depart and be with Christ, which is far better' (Phil 1:23). The 'do not awaken love before it pleases' points forward: the incarnation came 'in the fullness of time' (Gal 4:4), and the second coming waits for its own proper moment.

8.synthesis:
'The voice of my beloved! Behold, he comes, leaping over the mountains, bounding over the hills' (v. 8) — the exclamation of the beloved's approach is one of the most joyful moments in the Song. The beloved is not the passive object of the woman's longing but a figure of energetic, eager movement: he leaps, he bounds, he overcomes every obstacle to reach her. The mountains and hills are real geography transformed by love into mere obstacles to be cleared. He is compared to 'a gazelle or a young stag' (v. 9) — the same creatures by which the Song adjures — fleet, graceful, powerful, wild.

The window-scene of v. 9 — he is 'gazing through the windows, looking through the lattice' — introduces the threshold dynamic that runs through the Song: the beloved on one side, the woman on the other, the window or door or wall as the boundary between them. The tension between the desire to enter and the barrier that must be respected or negotiated is a recurring motif. Here, his gaze through the lattice is itself an act of devotion: he comes to see her even through the barrier, even before entry is possible.

The invitation of vv. 10–13 — 'Arise, my love, my beautiful one, and come away. For behold, the winter is past; the rain is over and gone. The flowers appear on the earth, the time of singing has come...' — is the Song's great spring poem. The invitation to 'arise and come away' is grounded in a seasonal renewal: winter is over, spring has come, the whole natural world is in bloom and singing. The beloved's invitation weaves together the beauty of the season and the urgency of love — just as the earth is renewed, so the time of love is here. The listing of sensory markers — flowers visible, turtle-dove cooing, fig tree budding, vines fragrant — creates an overwhelming case that the time is right, now, today.
8.historical_context: The gazelle and stag were standard love-poetry comparisons in the ancient Near East for the beloved's speed and beauty. The seasonal context of vv. 11–13 points to spring in Israel — after the rainy season (October–April), when the flowers bloom on the coastal plain and hill country, and the turtle-dove returns from its winter migration. The invitation to 'arise and come away' may reflect a formal invitation to leave the parental household for the beloved — a significant social transition in ancient Near Eastern marriage customs.
8.literary_structure: The section builds from distant anticipation (the voice heard, v. 8) → approach (leaping over mountains, gazelle comparison, v. 9) → arrival at the threshold (looking through the lattice, v. 9) → formal invitation (rise and come away, vv. 10–13) → the closing warning of the foxes (v. 15). The movement from distance to threshold to invitation enacts the spatial dynamics of the book: the beloved approaches but has not yet arrived, the invitation is extended but has not yet been accepted, and the little foxes that spoil the vineyards introduce a note of threat that complicates the pure joy of the spring poem.
8.theological_application: The great spring poem of vv. 10–13 — winter past, rain over, flowers appearing, singing time come — is the image of covenant renewal after judgment. The theological pattern: winter of exile, rain of affliction, then the spring of restoration in which YHWH again says 'arise and come away.' Isaiah uses identical imagery in the great servant songs: 'the former things have passed away; behold, I am doing a new thing' (Isa 43:18–19). The beloved's urgent invitation — it is the right time now, arise — is the shape of all divine call: meeting the person in the specific season of their life and saying that this moment is the moment of love's fulfillment.
8.christology: 'The voice of my beloved! Behold, he comes, leaping over the mountains' (v. 8) is the patristic image of the incarnation: the eternal Word leaping over the mountains of heaven to reach his beloved, taking on flesh to bridge the distance between divinity and humanity. Bernard of Clairvaux reads the mountains as the patriarchs and prophets over whom the Word 'leaped' on his way to the incarnation. The winter-past-spring-come poem is read eschatologically: the old creation's winter has passed, the new creation's spring has come through Christ's resurrection. 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation. The old has passed away; behold, the new has come' (2 Cor 5:17).

16.synthesis:
The final two verses of chapter 2 contain perhaps the Song's most famous declaration: 'My beloved is mine, and I am his; he grazes among the lilies' (v. 16). This formula of mutual belonging — 'mine... his' — will recur in modified form at 6:3 ('I am my beloved's and my beloved is mine') and 7:10 ('I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me'), with a significant progression: from equal mutuality (2:16) to the woman placing herself first in the belonging relationship (6:3) to the emphasis shifting to his desire for her (7:10). The formula's variations track a deepening of the relationship through the Song.

The closing invitation of v. 17 — 'Until the day breathes and the shadows flee, turn, my beloved, be like a gazelle or a young stag on the cleft mountains' — is temporally complex. The 'until the day breathes' (šeyāpûaḥ hayyôm) may refer to the cool breeze of evening or to the dawn; the 'mountains of Bether' (or cleft mountains) are unidentified geographically but image the separation that the night's coming will impose. The woman is both celebrating present belonging and anticipating the temporary separation of night — holding both in the same breath.
16.historical_context: The grazing-among-lilies image (v. 16) recurs at 6:3 and frames an erotic metaphor: the beloved grazes in the garden of his beloved (the lilies being associated with the beloved's beauty). The 'mountains of Bether' (or cleft mountains) of v. 17 is a geographic hapax — unique to this verse — suggesting an image of rocky inaccessibility rather than a specific location.
16.literary_structure: The mutual-belonging formula of v. 16 is the culmination of the chapter's movement: from spring invitation (vv. 10–13) through the little-foxes warning (v. 15) to the declaration of possession and belonging (v. 16). The chapter ends not with arrival but with a temporary holding: 'until the day breathes and the shadows flee' — as if the full union being sought is still slightly deferred, the shadows of separation still present until the dawn comes. The chapter ends in anticipation rather than arrival, the characteristic posture of the Song.
16.theological_application: The mutual-belonging formula — 'my beloved is mine, and I am his' (v. 16) — is the covenant formula of the Song: a declaration of reciprocal possession that mirrors the covenant language of the Torah: 'I will be your God and you will be my people' (Lev 26:12). The Song expresses in the language of erotic love what the covenant documents express in formal legal language: exclusive, mutual belonging. The progression of the formula through the Song — from his-then-mine to mine-then-his — reflects the maturing of a relationship in which increasing assurance gradually shifts the emphasis from what one receives to what one gives.
16.christology: The mutual-belonging formula finds its NT equivalent in the language of union with Christ: 'You are not your own; you were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19–20) and 'I am my beloved's' — the church belongs to Christ. But the mutuality is maintained: 'he who is joined to the Lord becomes one spirit with him' (1 Cor 6:17). The 'until the day breathes and the shadows flee' of v. 17 points to the eschatological dawn of the new creation: 'the night is far gone; the day is at hand' (Rom 13:12). The full union of Christ and his church awaits the coming of the day.
